topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien beneath my fingertips, a ghost of a life I never lived. I traced the elegant script, the carefully chosen anecdotes about my childhood, the supposed triumphs of my early career. Each sentence was a tiny, precise cut, slicing through the fragile remnants of my memory. A coldness settled deep in my chest, a heavy, suffocating weight that made drawing a full breath an effort. I reread the passage describing my greatest love, and a sharp, involuntary tremor ran through my hands, causing the paper to flutter slightly. I sank onto the edge of the antique desk, the sound of my own ragged exhale loud in the silence of the study. How could someone have woven such a convincing tapestry of my existence when I was simply… absent? My vision blurred at the edges, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically stop the world from tilting on its axis.

[story 2]

It was the discovery of the annotated manuscript that broke me. I found the draft of my autobiography tucked inside a forgotten box of old correspondence. The ink was familiar, yet the narrative was utterly foreign. The voice speaking about my ambitions, the descriptions of my anxieties—they belonged to someone else. A slow, agonizing pressure built behind my sternum, tightening until I felt breathless, as if the air itself had been withdrawn. I stood motionless for a long time, the room spinning slightly. When I finally moved, it was with a strange, detached slowness. I picked up a pen, the metal cool against my skin, and stared at the words as if they were venomous. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot, wet path down my cheek, and I scrubbed at it with a shaky hand, the effort feeling monumental. The silence of the apartment amplified the frantic hammering of my heart against my ribs.

[story 3]

The realization hit me not as a sudden shock, but as a slow, pervasive erosion of reality. I was reading my own life, meticulously documented, yet the core of it felt hollowed out, filled with borrowed light. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscle strain in my neck. I pushed the book away, the sound a soft thud that seemed impossibly loud in the quiet afternoon. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent cityscape, and felt a profound emptiness bloom in my core. My shoulders slumped, a complete surrender to the weight of the deception. I pressed my forehead against the cool glass, letting the sensation ground me, though the tremor in my hands betrayed the fragility of my composure. I didn't cry immediately; instead, a deep, hollow ache settled in my bones, a persistent, dull throb that made every movement feel heavy, weighted down by an unbearable sorrow.
